title: England's Buggery Act makes sodomy a capital crime
description: Henry VIII's Buggery Act made sodomy a criminal offence across England, punishable by death. It was the first statute to bring such prosecutions under secular rather than church courts.

title: Labouchere Amendment criminalises 'gross indecency' between men
description: Section 11 of the Criminal Law Amendment Act 1885, known as the Labouchere Amendment, made 'gross indecency' between men a crime in Britain. Courts interpreted it broadly to cover almost any intimate contact between men.

title: Attempt to criminalise sex between women fails in Britain
description: A proposal to extend the Labouchere Amendment to same-sex relationships between women was dropped by Parliament. Opponents argued that publicising the offence would draw attention to acts most women supposedly knew nothing about.

title: Wolfenden Committee set up to review homosexual offences
description: The Departmental Committee on Homosexual Offences and Prostitution, chaired by John Wolfenden, was established to examine the law on homosexuality. It followed prosecutions of prominent men including Lord Montagu and Michael Pitt-Rivers.

title: Wolfenden Report recommends decriminalising private acts
description: The Wolfenden Committee published its report, recommending that consensual homosexual behaviour between adults in private should cease to be a criminal offence. Parliament did not act on the recommendation at the time.

title: Homosexual Law Reform Society launches in Britain
description: The Homosexual Law Reform Society formed to campaign for changes to the laws criminalising homosexuality. It pressed for the reforms recommended by the Wolfenden Report.

title: April Ashley undergoes pioneering surgery in Casablanca
description: April Ashley, born in Liverpool in 1935, travelled to Casablanca where she became one of the earliest people to have gender reassignment surgery. She went on to become a well-known model and campaigner.

title: Sexual Offences Act decriminalises private acts between men
description: The Sexual Offences Act partially decriminalised sex in private between men aged over 21. Its reach was limited to England and Wales and excluded the Merchant Navy and the Armed Forces.

title: David Hockney wins the John Moores Painting Prize
description: The artist David Hockney took the sixth John Moores Painting Prize at the Walker Art Gallery in Liverpool. Hockney became one of Britain's most celebrated openly gay artists.

title: Stonewall Riots ignite the gay liberation movement
description: Patrons of the Stonewall Inn, a gay bar in Manhattan, fought back against repeated police raids, sparking days of unrest. The uprising became a catalyst for the American gay liberation movement and the founding of the Gay Liberation Front.

title: Gay Liberation Front founded in London
description: Aubrey Walter and Bob Mellors founded the British Gay Liberation Front, inspired by its American counterpart. It urged people to come out, be visible and challenge homophobia in wider society.

title: Britain's first official Gay Pride rally held in London
description: The first official Gay Pride rally in Britain took place in London, drawing around 700 people. The date was chosen to fall near the anniversary of the Stonewall uprising.

title: Scotland decriminalises private acts between men
description: The Criminal Justice (Scotland) Act extended decriminalisation to consensual homosexual acts in private between men aged over 21 in Scotland, more than a decade after England and Wales.

title: Northern Ireland decriminalises private acts between men
description: The Homosexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order decriminalised consensual sex in private between men in Northern Ireland, following a legal challenge that reached the European Court of Human Rights.

title: First AIDS cases recorded in the UK and USA
description: The earliest cases of what became known as AIDS were reported in Britain and the United States. The epidemic went on to reshape health policy and LGBTQ+ activism for decades.

title: Sunil Gupta highlights suppression of gay men in India
description: Marking 40 years of Indian independence, the artist Sunil Gupta observed that the anniversary also underlined the continued suppression of gay men in India, where sexuality remained absent from public debate.

title: Section 28 bans 'promotion of homosexuality' by councils
description: Section 28 of the Local Government Act took effect across England, Wales and Scotland, barring local authorities from intentionally promoting homosexuality or publishing material to that end. It became a rallying point for LGBTQ+ campaigners.

title: WHO stops classifying same-sex desire as a mental illness
description: The World Health Organization ceased defining same-sex attraction as a mental disorder. The change removed a long-standing medical justification for discrimination against LGBTQ+ people.

title: Age of consent for gay men lowered to 18 in England and Wales
description: The Criminal Justice and Public Order Act reduced the age of consent for gay men from 21 to 18 in England and Wales, while it stayed at 16 for heterosexual couples. Campaigners criticised the remaining inequality.

title: Filmmaker Derek Jarman dies of an AIDS-related illness
description: The filmmaker, artist and activist Derek Jarman died from an AIDS-related illness. His work and openness about his diagnosis made him a prominent figure in British queer culture.

title: UK bans workplace discrimination over gender reassignment
description: The Sex Discrimination (Gender Reassignment) Regulations made it unlawful for employers to discriminate against people because they are transgender. It was among the first legal protections for trans people in Britain.

title: Nail bomb attack on the Admiral Duncan pub in Soho
description: A nail bomb exploded at the Admiral Duncan, one of the oldest gay pubs in London's Soho, killing three people and injuring dozens more. The attack was part of a series targeting minority communities.

title: Caroline Paige transitions while serving in the British forces
description: Caroline Paige became the first officer in the British Armed Forces to transition gender while continuing to serve. Her case marked a step toward acceptance of trans personnel in the military.

title: UK lifts the ban on LGB people in the armed forces
description: The prohibition on lesbian, gay and bisexual people serving in the British Armed Forces was lifted, ending a policy that had led to dismissals and investigations of service members.

title: UK equalises the age of consent at 16 for everyone
description: The Sexual Offences Amendment Act set the age of sexual consent at 16 for all, removing the higher threshold that had applied to gay men. It brought the law into line for same-sex and opposite-sex couples.

title: Adoption and Children Act gives same-sex couples adoption rights
description: The Adoption and Children Act granted same-sex couples in England and Wales equal rights to adopt children as a couple, ending their exclusion from joint adoption.

title: Section 28 is repealed in England and Wales
description: After years of campaigning by LGBTQ+ groups, Section 28 was repealed, removing the ban on local authorities being seen to promote homosexuality. Scotland had abolished the provision three years earlier.

title: UK bans workplace discrimination based on sexual orientation
description: The Employment Equality (Sexual Orientation) Regulations made it unlawful to discriminate against workers because of their sexual orientation. It gave lesbian, gay and bisexual employees legal protection in the workplace.

title: Civil Partnership Act recognises same-sex couples
description: The Civil Partnership Act created a legal status for same-sex couples with rights and responsibilities close to those of civil marriage. Registrations began the following year.

title: Gender Recognition Act lets trans people change legal gender
description: The Gender Recognition Act allowed transgender people in Britain to obtain legal recognition of their gender and a new birth certificate. It was among the more far-reaching trans rights laws of its time.

title: Michael Causer killed in a homophobic attack in Liverpool
description: Michael Causer, a young gay man, was murdered in Liverpool after a homophobic assault. His death drew national attention to anti-LGBTQ+ violence.

title: Liverpool Pride founded after Michael Causer's murder
description: Liverpool Pride was established in response to the homophobic murder of Michael Causer two years earlier. It became the city's main annual LGBTQ+ celebration.

title: UK accepts its first LGBT+ refugees
description: Britain accepted its first refugees granted asylum on the basis of persecution for their sexual orientation or gender identity, recognising LGBTQ+ status as grounds for protection.

title: Same-sex marriage law passes in England and Wales
description: Parliament passed the Marriage (Same-Sex Couples) Act, providing for the legalisation of same-sex marriage in England and Wales. The law was set to take effect the following year.

title: Same-sex marriage takes effect in England and Wales
description: The Marriage (Same-Sex Couples) Act came into force, and the first same-sex weddings took place in England and Wales.

title: North Carolina passes anti-trans 'bathroom law'
description: North Carolina passed the Public Facilities Privacy and Security Act, requiring people to use public bathrooms matching the sex on their birth certificate. Other states, including Minnesota and Missouri, considered similar measures.

title: Mass shooting at the Pulse nightclub in Orlando
description: A gunman killed 49 people and injured 53 others at Pulse, a gay nightclub in Orlando, Florida. Vigils for the victims were held around the world, including in London, Manchester and Liverpool.

title: Women's March draws millions across the globe
description: The Women's March on Washington took place the day after the presidential inauguration, with sister marches in cities worldwide and an estimated five million participants. The protests championed women's rights, LGBTQ+ rights, racial equality and religious freedom.

title: Reports of an anti-gay purge in Chechnya
description: Reports emerged of the abduction and torture of up to 100 gay men held in camps in Chechnya, with at least three thought to have been killed. Chechen authorities denied the accounts as France accepted its first gay refugees from the region.

title: Same-sex marriage takes effect in Northern Ireland
description: Same-sex marriage laws came into force in Northern Ireland, making it the last part of the United Kingdom to allow same-sex couples to marry.
